heroOverview: Hematemesis is the vomiting of blood originating from the upper gastrointestinal tract — defined as any source proximal to the ligament of Treitz. The blood may appear bright red, indicating active or recent brisk bleeding, or dark red and coffee-ground in texture when it has been partially digested by stomach acid. Upper GI bleeding affects approximately 67-80 people per 100,000 per year in high-income countries, and hematemesis is its most dramatic presentation. The most common causes are peptic ulcers, ruptured esophageal varices, and Mallory-Weiss mucosal tears. Mortality from upper GI bleeding remains 2-15% overall, rising sharply with age, comorbidity, and delay in resuscitation. Prompt endoscopy — ideally within 24 hours of presentation — is both diagnostic and therapeutic in the majority of cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Upper GI bleeding affects 67-80 per 100,000 people per year; hematemesis is its most common presentation (NICE NG141, 2020)
keyStats.demographics: Rates peak in adults over 60; men are affected at approximately twice the rate of women
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age of presentation is approximately 65-70 years in most Western cohorts
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 300,000 hospital admissions annually in the United States relate to upper GI bleeding

definition: Hematemesis (ICD-10: K92.0) is the act of vomiting blood that has originated from somewhere in the upper gastrointestinal tract — the esophagus, stomach, or duodenum up to the ligament of Treitz. When bleeding is brisk and blood has spent little time in the stomach, the vomitus is bright red or maroon. When bleeding is slower or intermittent, gastric acid and pepsin partially digest the haemoglobin, producing the characteristic dark brown, granular appearance described as coffee-ground vomitus. Hematemesis is clinically significant because it signals upper GI bleeding (UGIB), which is a medical emergency until proved otherwise. It is distinguished from haemoptysis — coughing up blood from the lungs — by its association with nausea, its relation to swallowing, and the absence of frothy or bubbly character. Hematemesis falls under the care of gastroenterology, and when it arises from portal hypertension, hepatology is involved. The two broadest categories of UGIB are non-variceal, which accounts for roughly 80% of cases, and variceal, which is far less common but carries higher mortality. Within non-variceal bleeding, peptic ulcer disease is the single most frequent underlying diagnosis, responsible for approximately 35-50% of all acute UGIB episodes.

typesClassification.description: Indicates vigorous or ongoing bleeding, most often from a large vessel at the base of a peptic ulcer or from a ruptured esophageal varix; requires urgent resuscitation and early endoscopy.
Dark granular material resulting from partial digestion of blood by gastric acid; suggests slower or self-limiting bleeding but still warrants urgent assessment.
Bleeding from dilated submucosal veins in the esophagus or stomach, typically in the setting of portal hypertension from cirrhosis; carries mortality of 15-20% per episode.
Covers peptic ulcers, Mallory-Weiss tears, erosive gastritis, Dieulafoy lesions, and upper GI malignancy; constitutes about 80% of all cases.

primarySymptoms: Vomiting of red or maroon blood, which may be voluminous and alarming, marking the most conspicuous sign of significant upper GI bleeding.
Coffee-ground vomitus — dark, granular material resembling coffee grounds — indicating blood that has been partially digested in the stomach.
Melena — black, tarry, foul-smelling stools that develop when digested blood passes through the small intestine, often accompanying hematemesis when bleeding is significant.
Lightheadedness, dizziness, or near-syncope on standing, reflecting a fall in circulating blood volume and orthostatic hypotension.
Rapid heartbeat (tachycardia) and pallor as the body compensates for blood loss by increasing cardiac output.
Epigastric or upper abdominal pain preceding the vomiting, particularly when a peptic ulcer is the cause, though pain may be absent in elderly patients on NSAIDs.
Nausea and retching that precede the episode, which helps distinguish hematemesis from haemoptysis where coughing predominates.
Cold, clammy skin and sweating in moderate to severe bleeding, reflecting sympathetic activation and peripheral vasoconstriction.
Fatigue and weakness disproportionate to the vomiting alone, caused by the anaemia and haemodynamic changes produced by blood loss.
Abdominal distension or discomfort in patients with portal hypertension and ascites, where variceal bleeding is the likely source.
earlyWarningSigns: A single episode of dark coffee-ground vomiting even without accompanying symptoms.
Unexplained epigastric pain that suddenly worsens after a period of relative stability.
Finding black or dark tarry stools in the toilet before any vomiting occurs.
Feeling faint or unusually tired after taking NSAIDs, aspirin, or anticoagulants.
emergencySigns: Vomiting a large volume of bright red blood at any time — call emergency services immediately.
Loss of consciousness or collapse associated with vomiting blood.
Rapid heart rate above 100 beats per minute with sweating, pallor, and cold extremities — signs of haemorrhagic shock.
Blood pressure that falls significantly when moving from lying to standing (orthostatic drop ≥20 mmHg systolic).
Any hematemesis in a person known to have cirrhosis or portal hypertension, which signals the possibility of variceal bleeding.

causes.description: Gastric and duodenal ulcers cause 35-50% of all acute upper GI bleeds; ulcers erode into submucosal or deeper vessels, particularly the gastroduodenal artery in the posterior duodenal bulb, producing bleeding that ranges from slow oozing to torrential haemorrhage.

Portal hypertension — most often from alcoholic or viral cirrhosis — raises pressure in the portal venous system, forcing blood through dilated collateral veins in the esophageal submucosa that can rupture under pressure and produce life-threatening haemorrhage.

Forceful or repeated retching, coughing, or straining creates a longitudinal mucosal tear at the gastroesophageal junction; tears account for roughly 5-15% of UGIB and usually stop spontaneously, though active bleeding occasionally requires endoscopic treatment.

NSAIDs, alcohol, stress physiology in critically ill patients, and Helicobacter pylori damage the gastric or esophageal mucosa, producing multiple superficial erosions that bleed diffusely rather than from a single point; individually shallow but collectively significant.

A calibre-persistent submucosal artery — most commonly in the gastric fundus — that erodes through the overlying mucosa and bleeds massively without an associated ulcer; often missed on first endoscopy because the lesion is small and covered by a clot.

Gastric cancer and esophageal cancer can erode into blood vessels as tumours enlarge, causing chronic low-level or acute major bleeding; bleeding from malignancy accounts for roughly 1-5% of UGIB presentations but is disproportionately common in patients over 60 with weight loss or dysphagia.

A rare but catastrophic cause — an abnormal connection between the aorta or an aortic graft and the duodenum — that typically presents with a herald bleed followed by massive haemorrhage; it should be considered in any patient who has had previous aortic surgery.

riskFactors.description: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs inhibit prostaglandin synthesis, stripping the gastric mucosa of its protective layer; regular NSAID use increases the risk of peptic ulcer bleeding by approximately 3- to 5-fold, and the risk is further multiplied in combination with anticoagulants.

Heavy alcohol use causes erosive gastritis directly, and chronic alcohol-related cirrhosis leads to esophageal and gastric varices; acute alcohol ingestion also increases acid secretion and mucosal vulnerability.

H. pylori colonises the gastric antrum and disrupts the mucosal defence layer, causing peptic ulcers that bleed; eradication reduces recurrence of ulcer bleeding by more than 90% in H. pylori-positive patients.

Warfarin, direct oral anticoagulants, clopidogrel, and dual antiplatelet therapy all impair haemostasis and substantially increase the risk of a bleeding ulcer progressing to significant haemorrhage rather than sealing spontaneously.

Cirrhosis raises portal pressure and impairs hepatic synthesis of clotting factors, creating both the structural substrate for varices and a coagulopathy that impedes haemostasis; patients with cirrhosis have a 30% lifetime risk of a variceal bleed.

Older adults are more likely to have peptic ulcer disease, take NSAIDs for arthritis, and use anticoagulants for atrial fibrillation; they are also less able to mount a haemodynamic compensation, making bleeds more dangerous.
A history of UGIB, particularly from an ulcer that has not had H. pylori eradication or long-term acid suppression, predicts a high risk of rebleeding.
Oral corticosteroids alone modestly increase GI bleeding risk, and the combination of corticosteroids plus NSAIDs multiplies the risk approximately 15-fold compared with neither drug.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 60, who represent the majority of hospital admissions for upper GI bleeding
People with chronic liver disease or established cirrhosis from any cause
Regular users of NSAIDs, aspirin, or anticoagulants
Men, who have roughly twice the incidence of women for peptic ulcer-related bleeding
Heavy alcohol users, in whom both erosive gastritis and variceal bleeding are common
Critically ill patients in intensive care, where stress ulceration and gastric erosions are frequent
diagnosisOverview: Evaluation of hematemesis begins with simultaneous resuscitation and assessment — airway protection, large-bore intravenous access, fluid replacement, and urgent blood typing precede any investigation in an unstable patient. The Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS) is calculated immediately from readily available data — blood urea, haemoglobin, systolic blood pressure, heart rate, history of cardiac or liver failure, and the presence of melena or syncope — to stratify risk. A score of zero identifies a low-risk group that may be managed as an outpatient. For all others, the cornerstone of diagnosis is upper GI endoscopy (gastroscopy), performed within 24 hours of presentation in most patients and within 12 hours if haemodynamic instability persists despite resuscitation. Endoscopy identifies the bleeding source in 90-95% of cases and simultaneously allows haemostatic therapy. Blood tests include a full blood count, coagulation screen, renal function (raised urea disproportionate to creatinine strongly suggests UGIB as blood is digested as protein), liver function tests, and a cross-match. In patients with haematemesis and known or suspected liver disease, assessment of portal pressure, platelets, and INR guides variceal management. CT angiography is reserved for massive bleeding in whom endoscopy fails or is not immediately available, and it can localise the bleeding point before angiographic embolisation or surgery.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Stratifies risk of needing intervention or mortality at presentation, enabling safe outpatient management for the lowest-risk group.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician calculates a score using blood test results and clinical observations taken on arrival; no invasive procedure is needed for scoring.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly visualises the source of bleeding in the esophagus, stomach, or duodenum; allows simultaneous injection, clipping, banding, or thermal haemostasis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A flexible camera is passed through the mouth into the stomach under sedation; the procedure takes 10-20 minutes and typically finds and treats the bleeding point in a single session.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures haemoglobin to quantify blood loss; cross-match prepares compatible blood for transfusion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw; results are available within 30-60 minutes; the initial haemoglobin may underestimate acute blood loss before haemodilution occurs.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies coagulopathy from liver disease, anticoagulant therapy, or disseminated intravascular coagulation that will impair haemostasis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Part of the same blood draw; an elevated INR guides the need for fresh frozen plasma, vitamin K, or reversal agents before endoscopy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A raised blood urea disproportionate to serum creatinine is a reliable marker of significant upper GI bleeding, as luminal blood is absorbed and metabolised as protein.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Included in the routine blood panel; results within one hour.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Localises active bleeding at a rate of 0.5 ml/minute or higher in cases where endoscopy has failed or is not feasible, guiding interventional radiology or surgery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An iodinated contrast injection followed by a rapid CT scan of the abdomen; takes 5-10 minutes; the vascular team reviews the images to plan intervention.

treatmentOverview: Management of hematemesis follows a structured resuscitation-then-diagnosis-then-haemostasis pathway. Initial resuscitation targets a systolic blood pressure above 100 mmHg and a heart rate below 100 beats per minute using crystalloid fluids and, once cross-matched blood is available, packed red cells transfused to maintain haemoglobin at 70-80 g/L (80-90 g/L in patients with cardiovascular disease). A restrictive transfusion strategy improves outcomes in non-variceal bleeding and reduces portal pressure in variceal bleeding. Intravenous proton pump inhibitors — bolus then infusion — are started empirically to suppress gastric acid and stabilise clots before endoscopy; high-dose IV omeprazole or pantoprazole reduces rebleeding by about 40% in peptic ulcer bleeding. In suspected variceal bleeding, terlipressin or octreotide is given before endoscopy to lower portal pressure, alongside prophylactic antibiotics (typically ceftriaxone 1 g/day for 5 days) which reduce bacterial infections and improve survival. Endoscopy within 24 hours achieves haemostasis in 85-95% of actively bleeding lesions. For peptic ulcer haemorrhage, dual endoscopic therapy combining a local injection of diluted adrenaline with a mechanical clip or thermal probe reduces rebleeding to below 10%. Variceal bleeding is treated with endoscopic band ligation. When endoscopy fails or bleeding recurs, radiological embolisation (transcatheter arterial embolisation, TAE) achieves haemostasis in 75-90% of cases without general anaesthesia. Emergency surgery is reserved for refractory bleeding, with outcomes closely linked to the surgical centre's volume. After haemostasis, H. pylori testing and eradication, long-term PPI therapy for high-risk lesions, and structured review of anticoagulant and NSAID prescribing reduce the risk of recurrence.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous proton pump inhibitors (PPIs)
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose IV omeprazole or pantoprazole (80 mg bolus then 8 mg/hour infusion for 72 hours) neutralises gastric acid to stabilise clots over a bleeding ulcer and reduce rebleeding; continued as oral PPI therapy long-term.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: High-dose IV PPI reduces rebleeding by approximately 40% and need for surgery by 20-30% in peptic ulcer bleeding (Cochrane Review, Sreedharan et al., 2010).

medicalTreatments.name: Terlipressin or somatostatin analogues (octreotide)
medicalTreatments.description: Vasoconstrictors that reduce splanchnic blood flow and portal pressure; given before and during endoscopy in suspected variceal bleeding to facilitate haemostasis and reduce early rebleeding.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Terlipressin reduces 7-day mortality by approximately 34% in variceal bleeding compared with placebo (Cochrane meta-analysis).

medicalTreatments.name: Prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-course antibiotics reduce bacterial infections and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in cirrhotic patients with variceal bleeding; current guidelines recommend ceftriaxone 1 g/day for up to 5 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Antibiotic prophylaxis reduces risk of bacterial infection by approximately 60% and improves survival in cirrhotic patients with variceal haemorrhage (Fernandez et al., NEJM, 2016).

medicalTreatments.name: Red cell transfusion
medicalTreatments.description: Targeted to maintain haemoglobin at 70-80 g/L using a restrictive strategy; liberal transfusion worsens outcomes by raising portal pressure in cirrhosis and increasing viscosity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: A restrictive transfusion strategy (haemoglobin threshold 70 g/L) reduced mortality at 45 days by approximately 45% compared with liberal transfusion in one landmark RCT (Villanueva et al., NEJM, 2013).

medicalTreatments.name: H. pylori eradication therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Seven-day triple or quadruple antibiotic regimen (e.g. amoxicillin, clarithromycin, omeprazole) eliminates the primary cause of peptic ulcer disease; mandatory testing and eradication after any peptic ulcer bleed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: H. pylori eradication reduces peptic ulcer rebleeding from approximately 30% to under 3% over 12 months (Cochrane Review, Ford et al.).

medicalTreatments.name: Non-selective beta-blockers (propranolol, carvedilol)
medicalTreatments.description: Used as secondary prophylaxis after variceal bleeding to lower portal pressure; started once haemostasis is achieved, combined with repeat endoscopic band ligation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined pharmacological and endoscopic secondary prophylaxis reduces variceal rebleeding risk from approximately 60-70% to 30-40% over two years.

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency oversewing of bleeding ulcer
surgicalOptions.description: Open or laparoscopic ligation of a bleeding vessel at the ulcer base (e.g. gastroduodenal artery in posterior duodenal ulcer), typically with vagotomy and pyloroplasty; reserved for endoscopic failure.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Achieves haemostasis in approximately 90% of cases, but carries 10-20% operative mortality in the emergency setting, heavily influenced by age and comorbidity.

surgicalOptions.name: Transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS)
surgicalOptions.description: Interventional radiology procedure creating a shunt between portal and hepatic veins to decompress the portal system; used in variceal bleeding refractory to endoscopic and pharmacological treatment.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Controls acute variceal bleeding in 90-95% of cases; early TIPS (within 72 hours) reduces treatment failure and 1-year mortality in high-risk patients (Monescillo RCT).

alternativeTreatments.name: Transcatheter arterial embolisation (TAE)
alternativeTreatments.description: Interventional radiology technique that selectively occludes the bleeding artery with coils or gel-foam; increasingly used as an alternative to surgery when endoscopy fails, with lower procedural risk.

alternativeTreatments.name: Balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore tube)
alternativeTreatments.description: A temporising measure for massive variceal bleeding uncontrolled by endoscopy and pharmacology; an inflatable balloon compresses varices while definitive therapy is arranged. Superseded by self-expanding metal stents in many centres.

whySeeSpecialist: Hematemesis always warrants emergency hospital assessment. A gastroenterologist performs the diagnostic and therapeutic endoscopy that is the cornerstone of management. Patients with liver disease should also be seen by a hepatologist. Those with refractory bleeding require an interventional radiologist for embolisation or a surgical team for emergency procedures. After discharge, gastroenterological follow-up ensures H. pylori eradication is confirmed, PPI therapy is appropriately continued, and anticoagulant or NSAID prescribing is reviewed.
doctorSelectionGuide: Acute hematemesis is handled by emergency physicians, gastroenterologists, and, in variceal cases, hepatologists working as a team. After the acute episode, look for a gastroenterologist with expertise in therapeutic endoscopy, ideally in a unit with 24-hour endoscopy availability. For patients with cirrhosis, a hepatologist who participates in a multidisciplinary liver disease clinic offers coordinated management of portal hypertension, antibiotic prophylaxis, and consideration for liver transplantation.
preventionStrategies: Use the lowest effective dose of NSAIDs for the shortest period, and add a PPI if NSAID use is unavoidable in a person at ulcer risk.
Test for and eradicate H. pylori in anyone starting long-term NSAIDs, with a known peptic ulcer, or after an episode of UGIB.
Limit alcohol to within recommended limits; abstinence is advised once variceal bleeding has occurred.
For patients with cirrhosis and varices, start non-selective beta-blockers or perform prophylactic band ligation to reduce first-bleed risk.
Review anticoagulant therapy periodically; ensure patients are at INR goal, and co-prescribe a PPI when anticoagulants and antiplatelet agents are combined.
Treat all peptic ulcers to confirmed healing with follow-up gastroscopy for gastric ulcers to exclude malignancy.
lifestyleModifications: Abstain from alcohol, particularly in those with liver disease or a history of variceal bleeding.
Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin unless medically necessary; use paracetamol as a first-line analgesic.
Eat regular meals to buffer gastric acid and avoid prolonged fasting.
Stop smoking, which impairs mucosal healing and doubles the risk of peptic ulcer rebleeding.
Take prescribed PPIs consistently and do not stop them without medical advice.
Report any recurrence of black stools, vomiting, or epigastric pain to a clinician promptly.

recommended: Small, frequent meals to dilute gastric acid during the healing phase
Bland foods — cooked vegetables, lean protein, plain rice — during recovery from an acute bleed
Adequate hydration to support circulating volume during recovery
Iron-rich foods such as lean meat, legumes, and leafy greens to replenish iron stores after blood loss
avoid: Alcohol, which is directly toxic to the gastric mucosa and raises portal pressure
Spicy or highly acidic foods during the acute recovery period if they worsen epigastric discomfort
NSAIDs and aspirin as self-medication for pain after a GI bleed
Large infrequent meals that cause acid surges

exerciseGuidelines: Rest is essential during and immediately after an acute upper GI bleed. Return to normal activity should be gradual over one to two weeks as haemoglobin recovers. Heavy lifting and straining should be avoided for at least two weeks after endoscopic treatment of a large ulcer, as raised intraabdominal pressure can dislodge a haemostatic clot. Regular light-to-moderate exercise after full recovery supports liver health in those with alcohol-related disease.
prognosis: Outcome depends heavily on the cause, patient age, comorbidities, and the speed of treatment. Overall in-hospital mortality from acute upper GI bleeding is approximately 2-10% in low-risk patients and rises to 20-40% in those with cirrhosis, variceal bleeding, haemodynamic shock, or advanced cancer. The Rockall Score, calculated after endoscopy, accurately stratifies 30-day mortality risk. Peptic ulcer bleeding has an excellent outcome when treated promptly: rebleeding rates after dual endoscopic therapy are below 10%, and long-term recurrence is reduced to under 3% with confirmed H. pylori eradication. Variceal bleeding carries a higher short-term mortality of 15-20% per episode, but patients who survive and achieve secondary prophylaxis with beta-blockers plus band ligation have substantially improved long-term outcomes. Complete recovery of haemoglobin typically takes four to eight weeks.
recoveryTimeline: Most patients are observed in hospital for 24-72 hours after endoscopy. Low-risk lesions (clean-based ulcers, Mallory-Weiss tears that have stopped bleeding) may be discharged within 24 hours after oral re-feeding. High-risk stigmata (visible vessel, adherent clot) require 72-hour observation given a 15-30% rebleeding risk concentrated in the first three days. Anaemia from blood loss typically normalises over four to eight weeks with iron supplementation or dietary iron. Patients on anticoagulants should have a specialist review before restarting therapy, typically within one to two weeks.
complications: Rebleeding — occurs in 10-30% of patients depending on the lesion type; highest within the first 72 hours and concentrated in those with high-risk ulcer stigmata.
Haemorrhagic shock — sustained blood loss leads to end-organ hypoperfusion, acute kidney injury, and, in severe cases, multi-organ failure.
Aspiration pneumonia — vomiting blood into the lungs, particularly in patients with reduced consciousness, causes a chemical pneumonitis that can be life-threatening.
Iron-deficiency anaemia — significant blood loss depletes iron stores; untreated, it causes persistent fatigue, dyspnoea, and impaired cognitive function.
Acute kidney injury — hypovolaemia from blood loss reduces renal perfusion; patients taking nephrotoxic drugs such as NSAIDs are at particular risk.
Mortality — overall in-hospital mortality is 2-10% in unselected UGIB, rising to 15-20% in variceal bleeding episodes and higher in patients with decompensated cirrhosis or malignancy.

supportResources.name: American College of Gastroenterology — Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding
supportResources.description: Patient information on GI bleeding causes, treatment, and when to seek emergency care from a major US gastroenterology society.

supportResources.name: NHS — Vomiting blood (haematemesis)
supportResources.description: UK National Health Service plain-English guide to causes, what to do, and when to call an ambulance.

supportResources.name: British Liver Trust
supportResources.description: Support resources for patients with liver disease and portal hypertension, a major cause of variceal hematemesis.

sources.title: NICE NG141 — Acute upper gastrointestinal bleeding in over 16s: management (2020)
Villanueva C et al. Transfusion Strategies for Acute Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding. NEJM 2013
Laine L, Jensen DM. Management of Patients with Ulcer Bleeding. American Journal of Gastroenterology, 2012
de Franchis R, Bosch J et al. Baveno VII Consensus Recommendations on Portal Hypertension. Journal of Hepatology, 2022

confusedWithConditions.name: Melena
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Melena is black tarry stool from digested blood passed per rectum; hematemesis is vomited blood from the same upper GI source — they frequently coexist, but melena alone does not require vomiting.

confusedWithConditions.name: Colorectal cancer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lower GI sources produce fresh red rectal bleeding rather than vomited blood; colorectal cancer is distinguished by colonoscopy, not gastroscopy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peptic ulcer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A peptic ulcer is the underlying structural cause of hematemesis in 35-50% of cases; hematemesis is the bleeding complication, not the ulcer itself.

relatedConditions.name: Melena
relatedConditions.relevance: The two presentations commonly coexist when upper GI bleeding is brisk enough to produce both vomited and digested blood passed per rectum.

relatedConditions.name: Peptic ulcer disease
relatedConditions.relevance: The single most common cause of hematemesis, responsible for 35-50% of acute upper GI bleeding episodes.

relatedConditions.name: Portal hypertension
relatedConditions.relevance: Elevated portal pressure drives the development of esophageal and gastric varices, the second most common and most lethal cause of hematemesis.

relatedConditions.name: Acute gastric ulcer with hemorrhage
relatedConditions.relevance: A direct cause of hematemesis; acute gastric ulcers often develop in hospitalised or critically ill patients and bleed without prior symptoms.

faqs.question: What does it mean when you vomit blood?
faqs.answer: Vomiting blood means there is active bleeding somewhere in the upper digestive tract — the esophagus, stomach, or upper small intestine. It is always a medical emergency. Call emergency services immediately; do not wait to see if it stops.

faqs.question: What is the most common cause of vomiting blood?
faqs.answer: Peptic ulcer disease — gastric and duodenal ulcers — causes about 35-50% of all cases of upper GI bleeding presenting as hematemesis. The next most common causes are esophageal varices and Mallory-Weiss tears from forceful retching.

faqs.question: Is vomiting blood always an emergency?
faqs.answer: Yes. Vomiting any amount of blood requires same-day emergency assessment because the rate of bleeding can escalate rapidly and the underlying cause needs urgent identification and treatment. Call emergency services or go to the nearest emergency department.

faqs.question: What is coffee-ground vomiting?
faqs.answer: Coffee-ground vomiting is dark brown granular material that looks like coffee grounds. It forms when blood sits in the stomach long enough for gastric acid to partially digest the haemoglobin. It signals upper GI bleeding that may be slower than bright-red hematemesis but is still serious and requires emergency assessment.

faqs.question: How is vomiting blood different from coughing up blood?
faqs.answer: Vomiting blood (hematemesis) comes from the digestive tract; blood is mixed with food or bile, appears dark red or coffee-ground, and is preceded by nausea. Coughing up blood (haemoptysis) comes from the lungs; blood is bright red, frothy, and associated with coughing. Both are medical emergencies but have different causes and investigations.

faqs.question: What tests are done when you vomit blood?
faqs.answer: You will have blood tests — haemoglobin, clotting, kidney function, liver tests, and a cross-match for transfusion. A risk score (Glasgow-Blatchford Score) is calculated. The main diagnostic and treatment procedure is a gastroscopy, a camera examination of the stomach performed within 24 hours.

faqs.question: Can vomiting blood be treated with a camera procedure?
faqs.answer: Yes. Endoscopy (gastroscopy) is both diagnostic and therapeutic for most causes of hematemesis. Bleeding ulcers are treated by injecting adrenaline and clipping the vessel; varices are controlled with rubber-band ligation. Endoscopic haemostasis succeeds in 85-95% of cases.

faqs.question: What is the role of a proton pump inhibitor after vomiting blood?
faqs.answer: An intravenous PPI such as omeprazole is started before endoscopy and continued for 72 hours after to suppress acid, stabilise the blood clot over a bleeding ulcer, and reduce the 40% rebleeding rate. Long-term oral PPI is then continued, often indefinitely, for high-risk ulcers.

faqs.question: Does vomiting blood mean I have cancer?
faqs.answer: Stomach or esophageal cancer is a cause in roughly 1-5% of cases. The great majority of hematemesis is from peptic ulcers, varices, or Mallory-Weiss tears — all treatable. Gastroscopy examines the tissue directly and takes biopsies if any suspicious lesion is found, so cancer is ruled in or out at the same procedure.

faqs.question: Can aspirin and ibuprofen cause vomiting blood?
faqs.answer: Yes. NSAIDs including ibuprofen and naproxen, and aspirin even at low cardioprotective doses, damage the stomach lining and increase the risk of peptic ulcer bleeding by 3- to 5-fold. If you are on these medications and develop any upper abdominal pain, black stools, or vomiting blood, seek urgent medical attention.

faqs.question: What is a Mallory-Weiss tear?
faqs.answer: A Mallory-Weiss tear is a small split in the lining at the join between the esophagus and the stomach, caused by the mechanical force of forceful retching or vomiting. It typically causes bright-red hematemesis that appears after several bouts of non-bloody vomiting. Most tears stop bleeding on their own; a small proportion need endoscopic treatment.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from vomiting blood?
faqs.answer: Low-risk bleeding with a clean-based ulcer or Mallory-Weiss tear typically resolves within 24-72 hours of hospital treatment; most patients are discharged after one to three days. Anaemia takes four to eight weeks to fully correct. High-risk bleeding or variceal episodes require longer hospital stays and more intensive follow-up.

faqs.question: Why does blood in the stomach turn black in the stool?
faqs.answer: As blood passes through the small intestine, digestive enzymes and bacteria break down haemoglobin into haematin and other pigments that turn the stool jet black and give it a tarry, sticky consistency with a distinctive foul smell. This is called melena and indicates the bleeding source is in the upper GI tract.

faqs.question: Will I need a blood transfusion if I vomit blood?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Whether you need a transfusion depends on how much blood has been lost, your baseline haemoglobin, and your cardiovascular health. Current guidelines use a restrictive approach — transfusing only when haemoglobin falls below 70-80 g/L — as this improves outcomes compared with giving blood earlier.

faqs.question: What is the risk of vomiting blood in someone with cirrhosis?
faqs.answer: People with cirrhosis have a 30% lifetime risk of a variceal bleed, and each bleeding episode carries a 15-20% risk of dying within six weeks. Anyone with cirrhosis and hematemesis requires emergency admission, vasoactive drugs (terlipressin or octreotide), antibiotics, and urgent endoscopy with variceal band ligation.

faqs.question: Can I prevent vomiting blood from an ulcer?
faqs.answer: Yes. Testing and treating H. pylori reduces ulcer rebleeding from about 30% to under 3%. Avoiding NSAIDs or adding a PPI when they are essential, limiting alcohol, and stopping smoking all lower the risk. Patients who have had an ulcer bleed should take a long-term PPI and have H. pylori eradication confirmed.

faqs.question: What happens if endoscopy does not stop the bleeding?
faqs.answer: If endoscopy fails to control bleeding, interventional radiology can thread a catheter through a groin artery to the bleeding vessel and block it with a tiny coil — a procedure called transcatheter arterial embolisation. If that also fails, emergency surgery to oversew the vessel is performed. Variceal bleeding may need a TIPS procedure to lower portal pressure.

faqs.question: When can I restart blood thinners after vomiting blood?
faqs.answer: This depends on the cause of bleeding and the reason the anticoagulant was prescribed. For most patients with atrial fibrillation, anticoagulation is restarted within one to two weeks once haemostasis is confirmed, as the risk of stroke outweighs the rebleeding risk. Your gastroenterologist and cardiologist should discuss the timing together.

faqs.question: Is vomiting blood after drinking alcohol dangerous?
faqs.answer: Yes, and it warrants emergency evaluation. Forceful retching from alcohol can cause a Mallory-Weiss tear. In someone with alcohol-related liver disease, vomiting blood may signal variceal rupture — a life-threatening emergency. Any hematemesis after alcohol consumption should be assessed in an emergency department the same day.

faqs.question: Can stress cause vomiting blood?
faqs.answer: Severe physiological stress — major surgery, critical illness, severe burns, head injury — causes stress-related mucosal injury (stress ulcers) that can bleed. Psychological stress alone does not directly cause hematemesis, but it may worsen acid secretion. Patients in intensive care are given PPI prophylaxis specifically to prevent stress ulcer bleeding.

metaDescription: Hematemesis is vomiting blood from the upper GI tract, caused by peptic ulcers or varices. Learn emergency signs, diagnosis, and treatment options.
